summary:
Documentation & the Patient Care Report

The PCR is your most important document: it drives continuity of care, is a legal record, and supports billing, research, and QI.

"If it wasn't documented, it wasn't done." Write the PCR as if you'll defend every word in court. Documenting care you didn't give is falsification — a fireable, chargeable offense.

Fixing an error

Never erase, white-out, or alter a submitted PCR. Draw a single line through the error, initial and date it, and add the correction or a late-entry supplement.

What goes in it

Dispatch/times, demographics, chief complaint in the patient's words, history/MOI, past history-meds-allergies, baseline + serial vitals, exam findings, interventions and the patient's response, and transport details — woven into a narrative from dispatch to handoff.

Narrative formats

  • SOAP — Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan.
  • CHART — Chief complaint, History, Assessment, Rx (treatment), Transport.

Stay objective: write findings ("smelled of alcohol, slurred speech, unsteady gait"), not opinions ("appeared drunk"). Leave no blank fields — note "unable to obtain." Most services use ePCR; review for completeness before finalizing.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the Patient Care Report?
options: Continuity of care
Legal documentation
Determining hospital discharge diagnosis
Quality improvement

quiz.q: A PCR has been subpoenaed for a lawsuit. Which principle should guide your documentation practice?
options: It is acceptable to omit minor details
If it wasn't documented, it wasn't done
Only ALS interventions need to be documented
The PCR is not admissible in court

quiz.q: In the SOAP narrative format, what does the 'A' represent?
options: Actions taken
Assessment — your impression of the patient's condition
Administrative data
Arrival time

quiz.q: You notice an error in a written PCR you just completed. How should you correct it?
options: Use white-out and rewrite
Erase the error and write over it
Draw a single line through the error, initial and date it
Rewrite the entire PCR and discard the original

quiz.q: A patient is alert and oriented but refuses transport after a minor fall. What is the MOST important element to document?
options: That the patient was being difficult
That the patient understood the risks and was competent to refuse
Your opinion that the patient needed transport
That law enforcement was contacted
The patient was completely intoxicated
The patient is a frequent flyer who abuses the system
The patient had slurred speech and a strong odor of ethanol on his breath
The patient was a drunk mess

quiz.q: What is the PRIMARY advantage of electronic PCR (ePCR) over written PCR?
options: It allows you to document less information
It enforces mandatory fields and reduces omissions
It automatically completes the narrative for you
It cannot be used in court

quiz.q: A patient with chest pain is transported to the hospital. When should the PCR be completed?
options: Within 72 hours
Before the end of shift
As soon as possible after the call, ideally before taking the next call
Only if the patient requests it

quiz.q: Which field should be included in the minimum data set of every PCR?
options: Patient's insurance information
Patient's home address
Serial vital signs
Next of kin contact information

quiz.q: You are documenting a call involving a combative patient who required physical restraint. What should be included in your documentation?
options: Only that the patient was restrained
Detailed justification, type of restraints applied, duration, and serial assessment of neurovascular status
A statement that the patient was dangerous
The patient's criminal history

quiz.q: What does the 'H' in CHART narrative format stand for?
options: Patient history
Plan of care
Primary impression
Protocol used

quiz.q: A crew member asks if they can document a treatment they did not perform because 'it sounds better for the record.' What is your BEST response?
options: Agree, because the hospital will not check
Refuse — falsifying a PCR is illegal and unethical
Document it but note it was not performed
Document it only if they were too busy to perform it

flashcards.question: What is the legal maxim regarding documentation in EMS?
flashcards.answer: "If it wasn't documented, it wasn't done."

flashcards.question: What does the 'S' in SOAP narrative format stand for?
flashcards.answer: Subjective — the patient's chief complaint and history in their own words.

flashcards.question: How should you correct an error on a written PCR?
flashcards.answer: Draw a single line through the error, initial and date it, then write the correct information. Never use white-out or erase.

flashcards.question: What are the five functions of the PCR?
flashcards.answer: Continuity of care, legal document, billing/reimbursement, research data, and quality improvement.

flashcards.question: What does CHART stand for in narrative documentation?
flashcards.answer: Chief complaint, History, Assessment, Rx (treatment), Transport.

flashcards.question: What should you document when a patient refuses care?
flashcards.answer: Their competence (orientation), the risks explained (AMA form explained), their signature (or refusal to sign), and that ALS was not needed per protocol.

flashcards.question: Why is objective language important in a PCR?
flashcards.answer: Because subjective or judgmental language (e.g., 'patient was drunk') is unprofessional, inaccurate, and can be harmful in legal proceedings. Document objective findings instead.

critical.scenario: You and your partner respond to a 72-year-old male with chest pain. The patient is alert, oriented, and has a history of hypertension and diabetes. You perform a 12-lead ECG showing ST-elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. You administer oxygen, aspirin, and nitroglycerin en route to the hospital, with relief of pain from 8/10 to 4/10. Upon arrival at the ED, you give a verbal report to the nurse and transfer care. Later, back at the station, your partner starts writing the PCR and says, 'I don't remember the exact vitals from en route — I'll just make up something close enough.'
critical.question: What is the correct course of action regarding the missing vital signs?
options: Allow your partner to estimate the vitals since you both agree they were 'normal'
Leave the vital signs blank and submit the PCR as is

Document only the vital signs you are certain of and note that en route vitals were not recorded; then discuss the importance of accurate documentation with your partner and consider reporting the issue to your supervisor
Do not submit any PCR until you both can remember the exact numbers

critical.scenario: You are called to a single-vehicle MVC. The driver, a 34-year-old female, is ambulatory at the scene with minor abrasions and complains of neck pain. She refuses cervical collar and insists she is fine. She is alert and oriented × 3. She refuses transport and signs an AMA form. Two days later, you learn that the same patient was transported by another ambulance after developing severe neck pain and was diagnosed with a C5 fracture. Your supervisor asks to review your PCR for the original call.
critical.question: What element of documentation will be MOST critical in defending your care on the original call?
options: That you notified law enforcement of the refusal

Detailed documentation of the patient's mental status, the refusal risks explained, the patient's signature on the AMA form, and your assessment findings including the absence of neurological deficits
A note that the patient was 'difficult and uncooperative'
A statement that you recommended transport but the patient declined

critical.scenario: You respond to a motor vehicle collision with three patients: a 4-year-old child with a scalp laceration, a 35-year-old male with chest pain from airbag deployment, and a 70-year-old female complaining of hip pain. You are alone with one partner. You triage all three, call for additional resources, and begin care. After transport, you have three PCRs to complete. Another crew member asks if you can combine all three patients onto one PCR to save time.
critical.question: What is the most appropriate response?
options: Combine them since they were all from the same incident
Complete a separate PCR for each patient, ensuring patient identifiers match the correct assessment and treatment data
Document the child only since pediatric patients are highest priority
Skip documentation for the least injured patient
